text: Parenchymal transection during hepatectomy is arguably the most important and potentially dangerous step of the operation. Total control of the field during transection is critical to avoid intraoperative hemorrhage as well as postoperative biliary complications.
My name is Jason Hawksworth and I'm a transplant and hepatobiliary surgeon. In this video, I'll demonstrate how we perform parenchymal transection for a robotic right hepatectomy.

After achieving inflow control, the surgeon uses ultrasound to mark the middle hepatic vein, ensuring its preservation during the transection. The transection plane is marked, along with any crossing portal pedicles or hepatic vein branches.

The surgeon places the planned transection plane under tension and uses a combination of the laparoscopic CUSA and robotic vessel sealer to transect the liver parenchyma. Hemlock clips are applied to control larger vascular and biliary pedicles, while the CUSA allows for precise dissection of intrahepatic structures.

However, the disadvantage is that there is the need for a skilled bedside surgeon during these cases. Both the bedside surgeon and the surgeon at the robotic console work in concert to accomplish the parenchymal transection.
The surgeon encounters bleeding from a hepatic vein during the hepatectomy, which is controlled by packing the area with fibrillar material and applying pressure using a Raytech sponge.

The surgeon exposes and divides the caudate process using the laparoscopic CUSA, facilitating the dissection of the right hilar plate and the right bile duct. Indocyanine green (ICG) may be used to identify the right bile duct within the hilar plate. The right hilar plate is encircled with a silk tie and stapled using the robotic stapler.

During the parenchymal transection, coils from a preoperative portal vein embolization are visible. The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to divide the Heidler plate and continues the transection. The right hepatic vein is dissected and divided intraparenchymally with the robotic stapler, completing the parenchymal transection.

The surgeon uses the previously placed Raytec gauze lateral to the border of the inferior vena cava as a landmark to guide the transection plane. After completing the transection, an ultrasound with Doppler is employed to confirm the patency of the left hepatic artery and portal vein flow within the liver remnant.

The surgeon examines the transection plane of the liver remnant for hemostasis and potential biliary leakage. Indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence is utilized to assist in identifying any biliary leakage. The liver remnant may be pexied to prevent hepatic torsion.
Surgiflo is applied along the transection plane to achieve hemostasis. The surgical specimen is placed into a large endobag and extracted through the phanisteal incision.

Again, this is Jason Hawksworth demonstrating some key steps to a successful parenchymal transection during a robotic right hepatectomy. Thanks for watching and be on the lookout for upcoming videos on a variety of hepatobiliary minimally invasive procedures.
